questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for undirected graphs? P: Number of odd degree vertices is even. Q: Sum of degrees of all vertices is even. A. P only B. Q only C. Both P and Q D. Neither P nor Q
questions.to_find: Which of P and Q hold for every undirected graph

understand.plain: In any undirected graph every edge touches exactly two vertices, so it contributes 2 to the total degree count. That single fact forces the sum of all degrees to be even (statement Q), and from it you can show the odd-degree vertices must come in pairs, so their count is even too (statement P). Both statements are always true.

keywords.explain: The number of edges meeting at a vertex (a self-loop counts twice).
keywords.example: A vertex joined to 3 others has degree 3.

keywords.explain: The sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges, hence always even.
keywords.example: A triangle has degrees 2,2,2; sum = 6 = 2*3 edges.

keywords.explain: A vertex whose degree is an odd number; these must occur in an even count.
keywords.example: A path a-b-c has odd-degree ends a and c (two of them).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='70' x2='200' y2='40' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='200' y1='40' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='200' y1='130' x2='90' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='200' y1='130' x2='320' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='90' cy='70' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='200' cy='40' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='200' cy='130' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='320' cy='130' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='75' class='sub ink'>deg 2</text><text x='215' y='40' class='sub ink'>deg 2</text><text x='195' y='160' class='sub ink'>deg 3</text><text x='335' y='135' class='sub ink'>deg 1</text><text x='40' y='190' class='tag mut'>sum = 2+2+3+1 = 8 = 2 * (4 edges); odd-degree vertices = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A small graph with four edges whose degrees 2,2,3,1 add to 8 (twice the edge count) and whose odd-degree vertices number two.

given.aim: Decide whether P (even count of odd-degree vertices) and Q (even degree sum) are universally true.

terms.meaning: Degree of vertex v, the number of incident edges.
terms.example: deg(v) = 4 if four edges touch v.
terms.connects: Summed over all vertices it yields the total degree.

terms.meaning: Number of edges in the graph.
terms.connects: Each edge adds 2 to the degree sum.

terms.meaning: sum_{v} deg(v) taken over every vertex.
The set of vertices whose degree is odd.
terms.example: In degrees 3,1,2 there are two odd ones.
terms.connects: Their parity is forced by the even degree sum.

given.plan: Apply the Handshaking Lemma to get Q, then split the degree sum into odd and even parts to derive P.

steps.title: Sum of degrees is even (Q)
steps.apply: Every edge has two endpoints, so it is counted twice in the degree total. Thus the sum equals 2|E|, which is even regardless of the graph. Statement Q is TRUE.
steps.note: This holds for any undirected graph, with or without loops or multi-edges.

steps.title: Count of odd-degree vertices is even (P)
steps.apply: Split the degree sum into even-degree and odd-degree groups. The even group sums to an even number, and the whole sum is even, so the odd group must also sum to an even number. A sum of odd numbers is even only when there is an even count of them. Statement P is TRUE.
steps.note: So odd-degree vertices always pair up.

steps.title: Combine the verdicts
steps.apply: Both P and Q hold for every undirected graph, so the correct choice is C.
steps.note: Neither statement can fail, ruling out A, B and D.

questions.formula_note: The Handshaking Lemma (degree sum = 2|E|) is the key; it directly gives Q and, by a parity split, forces P.

questions.question: An ordered n-tuple (d_1, d_2, ..., d_n) with d_1 >= d_2 >= ... >= d_n is called graphic if there exists a simple undirected graph with n vertices having degrees d_1, d_2, ..., d_n respectively. Which one of the following 6-tuples is NOT graphic? A. (1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1) B. (2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2) C. (3, 3, 3, 1, 0, 0) D. (3, 2, 1, 1, 1, 0)
questions.to_find: Which degree sequence cannot be realised by a simple graph

understand.plain: A degree sequence is graphic if you can actually draw a simple graph (no loops, no repeated edges) whose vertices have exactly those degrees. The Havel-Hakimi test repeatedly connects the highest-degree vertex to the next highest ones and reduces their degrees; if you ever need a negative degree, the sequence is impossible. Tuple C fails this test because three vertices need degree 3 while only four vertices have any edges at all, which is too few to support them.

keywords.explain: A non-increasing degree list realisable by some simple graph.
keywords.example: (1,1) is graphic: a single edge between two vertices.

keywords.explain: Remove the largest degree d, subtract 1 from the next d entries, re-sort, repeat; reaching all zeros means graphic, a negative entry means not.
A graph with no self-loops and at most one edge between any pair of vertices.
keywords.example: A vertex can connect to at most n-1 others.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='28' class='lbl ink'>C: (3, 3, 3, 1, 0, 0)  Havel-Hakimi</text><text x='10' y='62' class='sub ink'>take 3 -> subtract 1 from next three: (3,3,1) -> (2,2,0)</text><text x='10' y='92' class='sub ink'>take 2 -> subtract 1 from next two: (2,0) -> (1, -1)</text><text x='10' y='126' class='tag no'>negative degree -1 reached => NOT graphic</text><line class='edge' x1='40' y1='165' x2='150' y2='165' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='40' cy='165' r='13' class='no'/><circle cx='150' cy='165' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='25' y='200' class='sub mut'>impossible to satisfy</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Havel-Hakimi reduction of (3,3,3,1,0,0) leading to a negative degree, proving it is not graphic.

given.aim: Identify the single 6-tuple that no simple graph can realise.

terms.meaning: A non-increasing list of six target degrees.
A necessary condition: the sum of degrees must be even.
terms.example: C sums to 10, which is even (so parity alone does not reject it).
terms.connects: Passing parity still requires the full Havel-Hakimi check.

terms.meaning: Vertices with non-zero target degree that can carry edges.
terms.example: In C only four vertices are active (degrees 3,3,3,1).
terms.connects: Too few active vertices to give three of them degree 3.

terms.meaning: What remains after one Havel-Hakimi step.
terms.example: C reduces toward (1,-1), an invalid degree.

given.plan: Run the Havel-Hakimi reduction on each candidate; the one that produces a negative degree is NOT graphic.

steps.title: Check the easy ones first
steps.formula_raw: drop d_1, subtract 1 from next d_1 terms, re-sort, repeat
steps.apply: A = (1,1,1,1,1,1) is three disjoint edges, graphic. B = (2,2,2,2,2,2) is a 6-cycle, graphic. D = (3,2,1,1,1,0) reduces (3,2,1,1,1) -> (1,0,0,1) -> sortable to all zeros, graphic.
steps.note: All three are realisable, so the answer must be C.

steps.title: Reduce tuple C
steps.apply: Drop the leading 3 and subtract 1 from the next three entries (3,3,1): they become (2,2,0), giving (2,2,0,0,0). Drop a 2 and subtract 1 from the next two (2,0): they become (1,-1). A degree of -1 is impossible.
steps.note: Intuitively, three vertices each need degree 3 but only four vertices ever carry edges, which cannot all be satisfied.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Only C fails the realisability test, so the answer is C.
steps.note: Note its degree sum 10 is even, so the parity check alone would not have caught it.

questions.formula_note: Havel-Hakimi is the decisive tool; an even degree sum is necessary but not sufficient, which is exactly the trap option C sets.

questions.question: G is an undirected graph with n vertices and 25 edges such that each vertex of G has degree at least 3. Then the maximum possible value of n is _________ .
questions.to_find: The largest number of vertices n possible under the constraints

understand.plain: The total degree is fixed: with 25 edges the degrees of all vertices add up to 2 * 25 = 50. If every vertex must have degree at least 3, then more vertices means more degree is required, so the degree budget of 50 limits how many vertices you can have. Dividing 50 by the minimum degree 3 gives the cap, and you take the largest whole number that fits.

keywords.explain: The smallest degree among all vertices, written delta(G); here delta(G) >= 3.
keywords.example: If every vertex has degree 3 or more, delta(G) = 3 at least.

keywords.explain: The total of all degrees equals 2|E|, a fixed amount to share out.
keywords.example: 25 edges give a budget of 50 degree-units.

keywords.explain: n must be a whole number, so a fractional bound is rounded down.
keywords.example: 50/3 = 16.67, so n can be at most 16.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='34' class='lbl ink'>degree sum = 2|E| = 2 * 25 = 50</text><text x='10' y='72' class='sub ink'>each vertex needs deg >= 3, so 3n <= 50</text><text x='10' y='104' class='sub ink'>n <= 50/3 = 16.67</text><text x='10' y='142' class='big ok'>n_max = 16</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The degree sum 50 divided by the minimum degree 3 gives 16.67, so the maximum number of vertices is 16.

given.aim: Maximise the vertex count n given 25 edges and minimum degree 3.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices, the quantity to maximise.
terms.example: We test how large n can be.
terms.connects: Bounded above by the degree-sum budget.

terms.meaning: Fixed edge count of the graph.
Every vertex has degree at least 3.
terms.example: No vertex may have degree 0, 1 or 2.

terms.meaning: Total of all vertex degrees.

given.plan: Use the minimum-degree bound delta * n <= degree sum = 2|E|, then take the integer floor.

steps.title: Fix the degree sum
steps.apply: With 25 edges the sum of all degrees equals 50; this is the total degree available to distribute among the vertices.
steps.note: This total is constant whatever the structure.

steps.title: Bound n with the minimum degree
steps.apply: Since every vertex has degree at least 3, the degree sum is at least 3n. Combined with the total 50: 3n <= 50, so n <= 50/3 = 16.67.
steps.note: Larger minimum degree would shrink the cap further.

steps.title: Take the integer maximum
steps.apply: n must be a whole number, so n <= 16. n = 16 is achievable: give fifteen vertices degree 3 and one vertex degree 5 (sum = 45 + 5 = 50), which a valid simple graph can realise. Hence n_max = 16.
steps.note: The realisable construction confirms 16 is attainable, not merely an upper bound.

questions.formula_note: The minimum-degree bound delta * n <= 2|E| is key: it turns 'degree at least 3' into 3n <= 50, capping n at 16.

questions.question: The minimum number of colours required to colour the vertices of a cycle with n nodes in such a way that no two adjacent nodes have the same colour is A. 2 B. 3 C. 4 D. n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2
questions.to_find: The chromatic number of a cycle on n nodes

understand.plain: A cycle is a ring of n vertices, each joined to its two neighbours. If n is even you can alternate two colours all the way round, so 2 colours suffice. If n is odd, alternating fails when you close the loop, so you need a third colour. The only option that gives 2 for even n and 3 for odd n is the formula in D.

keywords.explain: The fewest colours needed so that adjacent vertices differ in colour, written chi(G).
A graph of n vertices arranged in a loop, each adjacent to exactly two others.
keywords.example: A triangle is the cycle C_3.

keywords.explain: Even cycles are bipartite (2-colourable); odd cycles need 3 colours.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='40' y='24' class='lbl ink'>even cycle C_4 = 2 colours</text><text x='270' y='24' class='lbl ink'>odd cycle C_5 = 3 colours</text><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='70' x2='160' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='70' x2='160' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='140' x2='90' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='140' x2='90' y2='70' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='90' cy='70' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='70' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='140' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='90' cy='140' r='12' class='ok'/><line class='edge' x1='350' y1='55' x2='405' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='405' y1='95' x2='385' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='385' y1='160' x2='315' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='315' y1='160' x2='295' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='295' y1='95' x2='350' y2='55' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='350' cy='55' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='405' cy='95' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='385' cy='160' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='315' cy='160' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='295' cy='95' r='12' class='no'/><text x='30' y='205' class='tag mut'>D: n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2 = 2 (even n), 3 (odd n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An even 4-cycle coloured with two colours and an odd 5-cycle needing a third colour, matching the formula in option D.

given.aim: State the chromatic number of a cycle C_n for general n.

terms.meaning: Number of nodes on the cycle.
Each node is joined only to its two cycle neighbours.
The integer part of n/2 used in option D.
Chromatic number of the cycle.

given.plan: Find chi(C_n) by parity, then match it to the option that yields 2 for even n and 3 for odd n.

steps.title: Even cycle
steps.apply: Colour the nodes alternately colour-1, colour-2 around the ring. Since n is even, the last node (colour-2) sits next to the first (colour-1), so no clash. Two colours suffice.

steps.title: Odd cycle
steps.apply: With n odd, alternating two colours leaves the first and last node sharing a colour while adjacent, a clash. One extra colour for the last node fixes it, so exactly 3 colours are needed.
steps.note: Odd cycles are the smallest non-bipartite obstacles.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.apply: Test option D: for even n, n - 2*(n/2) + 2 = 0 + 2 = 2; for odd n, n - 2*((n-1)/2) + 2 = 1 + 2 = 3. This is the only choice covering both cases, so the answer is D.
steps.note: Fixed numbers A, B, C cannot be right for all n.

questions.formula_note: chi(C_n) is 2 for even n and 3 for odd n; option D's expression n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2 reproduces exactly this, which is why a constant answer is wrong.

questions.question: The minimum number of colours required to colour the following graph, such that no two adjacent vertices are assigned the same color, is A. 2 B. 3 C. 4 D. 5 (The graph has a top apex and a bottom apex, each joined to all four vertices of a central square; the central square's four vertices form a 4-cycle.)
questions.to_find: The chromatic number of the given graph

understand.plain: Look for the largest set of vertices that are all mutually connected, because that clique forces at least that many colours. In this figure the top apex, the bottom apex, and two adjacent square vertices form a group of four vertices that are all pairwise joined (a K4). That forces at least 4 colours, and a careful colouring shows 4 colours are also enough, so the answer is 4.

keywords.explain: A set of vertices that are all pairwise adjacent; a clique of size k needs k colours.
keywords.example: A triangle K3 is a clique needing 3 colours.

keywords.explain: chi(G) is at least the size of the largest clique omega(G).
keywords.example: If G contains K4 then chi(G) >= 4.

keywords.explain: An assignment of colours so adjacent vertices differ; the goal is to use as few as possible.
keywords.example: Colour apexes and square so no edge has matching ends.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 360' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='35' x2='110' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='35' x2='210' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='35' x2='70' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='35' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='325' x2='110' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='325' x2='210' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='325' x2='70' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='325' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='140' x2='210' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='140' x2='250' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='250' y1='180' x2='210' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='210' y1='220' x2='110' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='220' x2='70' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='180' x2='110' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='160' cy='35' r='13' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='325' r='13' class='ok'/><circle cx='110' cy='140' r='13' class='no'/><circle cx='210' cy='140' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='250' cy='180' r='13' class='no'/><circle cx='210' cy='220' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='110' cy='220' r='13' class='no'/><circle cx='70' cy='180' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='35' y='352' class='tag mut'>contains K4 => chi = 4 colours</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A graph with two apex vertices each joined to a central 4-cycle; it contains a K4, so it is 4-chromatic.

given.aim: Determine the chromatic number of the depicted graph.

terms.meaning: Two special vertices, each adjacent to all four square vertices.
terms.example: The apex joins every middle vertex.
terms.connects: Each apex plus two adjacent square vertices forms a K4.

terms.meaning: Four vertices forming a 4-cycle in the middle.
terms.example: The square's corners are pairwise adjacent only along its edges.
terms.connects: Adjacent square corners join apexes to make a clique of 4.

terms.meaning: Size of the largest clique in G.
Chromatic number, the answer we seek.
terms.connects: Matches the clique bound and an explicit 4-colouring.

given.plan: Find the largest clique to lower-bound chi(G), then exhibit a valid 4-colouring to show 4 is enough.

steps.title: Locate a K4
steps.apply: Take the top apex T, the bottom apex B, and two adjacent square vertices u and v. T and B are each joined to u and v; u and v are joined by a square edge; and T-B... the two apexes share both u and v as common neighbours. T, u, v plus either apex give a set of four mutually adjacent vertices, a K4. So at least 4 colours are needed.
steps.note: A clique of size 4 cannot be coloured with fewer than 4 colours.

steps.title: Colour with exactly 4
steps.apply: Assign colour 1 to the top apex, colour 2 to the bottom apex. Around the square use colours 3,4,3,4 in cycle order so adjacent corners differ; each corner also differs from both apexes (colours 1,2). No edge has matching endpoints, so 4 colours suffice.
steps.note: Lower bound 4 plus a valid 4-colouring pins chi(G) = 4.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Since 4 colours are both necessary and sufficient, the minimum number of colours is 4, giving answer C.
steps.note: 5 colours (option D) are never needed; 2 or 3 are too few for the K4.

questions.formula_note: The clique bound chi(G) >= omega(G) is key: spotting the K4 forces at least 4 colours, and an explicit 4-colouring shows 4 is exact.

formulas.name: Havel-Hakimi Test for Graphic Sequences
formulas.plain: Sort degrees non-increasing; remove the largest degree d_1 and subtract 1 from the next d_1 entries; re-sort and repeat. All zeros => graphic; a negative entry => not graphic.
formulas.whenToUse: To decide whether a given degree sequence can be realised by a simple undirected graph.

terms.means: the largest (first) degree in the sorted non-increasing sequence
the i-th degree in the sequence, reduced as the algorithm proceeds
number of vertices (length of the sequence)

formulas.trap: An even degree sum is necessary but NOT sufficient; a sequence can pass the parity check yet still fail Havel-Hakimi.
formulas.reference: Havel (1955); Hakimi (1962); standard graph theory degree-sequence realisability.

formulas.name: Chromatic Number of a Cycle C_n
formulas.plain: A cycle on n vertices needs 2 colours when n is even and 3 colours when n is odd; equivalently chi(C_n) = n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2.
formulas.whenToUse: To find the minimum number of colours for a proper vertex colouring of a single cycle.

terms.means: the cycle graph on n vertices
number of nodes on the cycle
chromatic number, the fewest colours for a proper colouring

formulas.trap: Do not assume 2 colours always work; odd cycles close the loop on a clash and require a third colour.
formulas.reference: Standard result: even cycles are bipartite, odd cycles are not.

formulas.name: Clique Lower Bound on Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique; if G contains a K_k then at least k colours are needed.
formulas.whenToUse: To lower-bound the number of colours needed; spotting a K_k forces chi(G) >= k.

terms.means: clique number, the size of the largest complete subgraph
complete graph on k mutually adjacent vertices

formulas.trap: The bound is only a lower bound; chi(G) can exceed omega(G), so always confirm sufficiency with an explicit colouring.
